reasoning_model_1: The model interprets the first word 'Hiccup!' as a sound effect rather than text to be read aloud, replacing it with a breathy, laugh-like sound at the beginning (0:00-0:02). While this indicates an attempt to generate non-linguistic vocalizations, the sound resembles a giggle or gasp more than a genuine hiccup. Furthermore, the model fails to sustain the effect; despite the text stating 'I can't stop hiccuping,' the remainder of the speech is perfectly smooth and fluent without any further interruptions or hiccup sounds. The instruction to read 'with hiccups' (plural/continuous) was largely missed, resulting in a very limited expression of the feature.
reasoning_model_2: The model strictly follows the text script as a standard reading task. It reads the word 'Hiccup' literally at the beginning (0:00-0:01) rather than performing the sound of a hiccup. Throughout the rest of the audio, the delivery is smooth, professional, and completely devoid of any non-linguistic vocalizations or interruptions. Consequently, it fails entirely to meet the core requirement of the prompt to read the sentence 'with hiccups'.
model_comparison: The user's command specifically requested the generation of 'Non-Linguistic Vocalizations' (hiccups) while reading the text. Model 1 attempted this by converting the initial onomatopoeia 'Hiccup!' into an actual vocal sound, although the execution was poor (sounding like a giggle) and lacked consistency throughout the sentence. Model 2, however, completely ignored the paralinguistic instruction, reading the text literally and fluently without any simulated hiccups. Since Model 1 engaged with the core constraint of the prompt—generating a non-linguistic sound—while Model 2 did not, Model 1 is the superior model in this specific context, despite its imperfect performance.
